title: Sentence — The Grammar Family
summary: A sentence is a group of words which makes complete sense. Learn the Grammar Family story — Mr. Noun, Mrs. Verb, and their children — to understand how words work together.

content.definition: A sentence is a group of words which makes complete sense. Letters make words, words make sentences, and sentences make communication.
key_points: Every sentence has a Subject (who/what) and a Predicate (what about it)
The Verb is the action or state of being in a sentence
The Object receives the action done by the subject
A Complement completes the meaning — subject complement or object complement
An Adjunct provides additional information about the verb (can be removed)

patterns.example: Birds fly.
John helps friends.
Cobra is dangerous.
Children grow fast.
They elected him chairman.
He gave her a present.
patterns.explanation: Subject + Verb + Indirect Object + Direct Object

content.grammar_family: There is a family in London whose surname is Grammar. Mr. Noun and Mrs. Verb are the couple. They have three children: son Pronoun, and daughters Adjective and Adverb. Pronoun does all his father's work in his absence. Adjective loves her father (Noun) and keeps praising him. Adverb loves her mother (Verb) and always modifies her. Preposition is the chief servant, Conjunction is the matchmaker who joins words, and Interjection is the function supplier for Joy, Surprise, and Sorrow.

kinds_of_sentences.description: Shows a wish or prayer for someone or something.

title: Parts of Sentence & Patterns
summary: Master the six building blocks — Subject, Verb, Object, Complement, Adjunct — and recognize sentence patterns like SVO, SVC, SVOC, and SVIODO.

content.definition: Every sentence can be broken into parts that play specific roles. Understanding these parts helps you construct correct and varied sentences.
key_points: Subject — the person or thing doing the action: 'Birds fly in the sky'
Predicate — the part that describes what the subject does: 'Birds fly in the sky'
Verb — the action or state word: 'Birds fly in the sky'
Object — receives the action: 'John helps friends'
Complement — completes the meaning: 'India is a country' (subject complement)
Adjunct — optional extra info: 'Birds fly in the sky'

patterns.example: Sun shines.
Cobra is a snake.
Birds build nests.
She plays music actively.
The teacher gave him a present.
They elected him new chairman.

title: Subject-Verb Agreement
summary: The subject and verb must agree in number. Learn the rules that govern singular/plural agreement, collective nouns, and tricky cases.

content.definition: Subject-verb agreement means the verb must match the subject in number (singular or plural). Getting this right is essential for correct English.
key_points: Singular subject takes singular verb: 'He plays cricket'
Plural subject takes plural verb: 'Girls sing songs'
Phrases between subject and verb don't change agreement: 'The minister, along with his followers, is visiting'
Two subjects joined by 'and' take plural verb: 'Seetha and Geetha are good friends'
Either/Neither...or/nor: verb agrees with the nearest subject
Collective nouns can be singular or plural depending on meaning: 'The committee takes a decision' vs 'The committee are divided'

patterns.example: Everyone has to obey the law.
Mathematics is my favourite subject.
patterns.explanation: Subject names ending in -s but singular meaning

patterns.example: Hundred rupees is not a big amount.
The United States of America is a rich country.
Some of the books are found spoilt.
Some of the music is not audible.

title: Verb Classification & Usage
summary: Verbs are the heart of every sentence. Learn to classify verbs into auxiliaries, modals, finite, non-finite, transitive, intransitive and more.

content.definition: A verb is a word which denotes an action or a state of being. Verbs are classified into several types based on their function in a sentence.
key_points: Auxiliary verbs help the main verb: Be forms (am, is, are, was, were), Have forms (has, have, had), Do forms (do, does, did)
Modal verbs show ability, permission, obligation: can, could, may, might, shall, should, will, would, must
Action verbs show what the subject does: 'He plays cricket'
Linking verbs connect subject to complement: 'She is happy'
Transitive verbs need an object: 'He kicked the ball'
Intransitive verbs don't need an object: 'Birds fly'

patterns.example: Am, Is, Are, Was, Were, Be, Been, Being
patterns.explanation: Used for continuous tenses and passive voice

patterns.example: He has taken lunch before the meeting.
Does she go to market?
patterns.explanation: Used for questions and negatives in simple tenses

patterns.example: You should study hard.
Kohli plays cricket.
patterns.explanation: Verb needs an object to complete meaning

patterns.example: Sun shines brightly.
patterns.explanation: Verb is complete without an object

title: Modals & Semi-Modals
summary: Modal verbs express necessity, possibility, permission and ability. Learn can/could, may/might, shall/should, will/would, must, and semi-modals.

content.definition: Modal auxiliary verbs are used with main verbs to express ability, possibility, permission, obligation, and prediction. They don't change form for person or number.
key_points: Can — ability/permission: 'She can swim well'
Could — past ability/polite request: 'Could you help me?'
May — permission/possibility: 'It may rain today'
Might — less certain possibility: 'He might come tomorrow'
Shall — future/suggestion: 'Shall we go?'
Should — advice/obligation: 'You should study hard'
Must — strong obligation/certainty: 'You must obey the rules'
Semi-modals: need to, have to, ought to, used to, dare, be able to

patterns.example: She can speak three languages.
May I come in?
You must wear a helmet.
You should eat healthy food.
It might rain tomorrow.
Would you please close the door?

title: Non-Finites: Gerunds, Infinitives & Participles
summary: Non-finite verbs don't change with tense or person. Master gerunds (-ing as noun), infinitives (to + verb), and participles (verb as adjective).

content.definition: Non-finite verbs (also called verbals) are verb forms that don't act as the main verb. They function as nouns, adjectives, or adverbs in a sentence.
key_points: Gerund = verb + ing used as a noun: 'Swimming is good exercise'
Infinitive = to + verb: 'She wants to learn English'
Present participle = verb + ing used as adjective: 'The running water is clean'
Past participle = verb in -ed/-en form as adjective: 'The broken window needs repair'
Gerunds follow certain verbs: enjoy, avoid, consider, finish, practice
Infinitives follow certain verbs: want, decide, hope, plan, agree

patterns.example: Swimming is good exercise.
He enjoys reading books.
She studies to pass the exam.
The boy sitting there is my friend.
The car parked outside is mine.
He seems to have left already.

title: Phrasal & Causative Verbs
summary: Phrasal verbs combine a verb with a preposition/adverb to create new meaning. Causative verbs show that someone causes another to do something.

content.definition: Phrasal verbs are combinations of a verb with one or more particles (preposition/adverb) that create a meaning different from the original verb.
key_points: Phrasal verbs often have idiomatic meanings: 'give up' ≠ 'give' + 'up'
Separable: object can go between verb and particle: 'Turn off the light' or 'Turn the light off'
Inseparable: object must follow the particle: 'Look after the children'
Causative verbs: make, have, let, get — someone causes an action
'Make' implies force: 'The teacher made him stand'
'Let' implies permission: 'Let me go'

patterns.example: She gave up smoking.
Please look after my dog.
He turned down the offer.
The teacher made students write essays.
She had her car repaired.
He got his friend to help him.

title: Articles — A, An, The
summary: Articles (A, An, The) are small but critical. Learn when to use indefinite articles (a/an), the definite article (the), and when to omit them entirely.

content.definition: Articles are determiners that precede nouns. English has two types: indefinite (a, an) for general reference and definite (the) for specific reference.
key_points: 'A' is used before consonant sounds: a book, a university (sounds like 'yoo')
'An' is used before vowel sounds: an apple, an hour (silent 'h')
'The' refers to something specific or already known: 'The sun rises in the east'
No article with uncountable/abstract nouns in general: 'Water is essential'
No article with proper nouns (usually): 'India is a country'
Use 'the' with superlatives: 'the best', 'the tallest', 'the most beautiful'

patterns.example: He is a teacher.
She ate an orange.
The Ganga is a holy river.
He is the best player.
Gold is a precious metal.
A European, An MBA
patterns.explanation: Sound matters, not spelling — European starts with 'y' sound

title: Parts of Speech — Overview
summary: Every word in English belongs to one of 8 parts of speech: Noun, Pronoun, Verb, Adjective, Adverb, Preposition, Conjunction, Interjection.

content.definition: Parts of speech classify words based on their function in a sentence. A word can serve as different parts of speech in different contexts — these are called Versonyms.
key_points: Noun — names a person, place, thing or idea: 'Mumbai is a city'
Pronoun — replaces a noun: 'He is a teacher'
Verb — shows action or state: 'Birds fly'
Adjective — describes a noun: 'The tall building'
Adverb — modifies a verb, adjective, or adverb: 'She runs quickly'
Preposition — shows relationship: 'The book is on the table'
Conjunction — joins words/clauses: 'Ram and Sita'
Interjection — expresses emotion: 'Wow! That's amazing'

patterns.example: WATER — noun: 'Water is colourless' / verb: 'Water the plants'
patterns.explanation: Same word, different parts of speech

patterns.example: FAST — noun: 'Muslims observe fast' / adjective: 'He is a fast runner' / adverb: 'Don't drive fast'
MAKE — noun: 'It is a make of yesteryears' / verb: 'Make a sentence'
patterns.explanation: Context determines the part of speech

patterns.example: The army is marching forward.
Everyone has to obey the law.
The cat is under the table.

title: Nouns — Classification & Kinds
summary: Nouns are the naming words. Learn gender, concrete vs abstract, countable vs uncountable, proper, common, collective, compound, and possessive nouns.

content.definition: Nouns are words used for person, place, thing or animal. They are classified by gender, form (concrete/abstract), countability, and specificity.
key_points: Masculine Gender — refers to males: Boy, Son, King
Feminine Gender — refers to females: Mother, Daughter, Queen
Common Gender — both male and female: Student, Teacher, Worker
Neuter Gender — lifeless things: Water, Wood, Table
Proper Noun — specific: English, Tokyo, Nelson Mandela
Common Noun — general: student, city, country
Collective Noun — group: army, family, committee
Abstract Noun — intangible: love, help, fast
Compound Noun — multiple words: mother-in-law, kick-start

patterns.example: English is a global language.
Every student must have discipline.
The army is marching forward.
Love is an emotion.
Water is colourless.
My mother-in-law is a nice lady.

title: Pronouns — All Kinds
summary: Pronouns replace nouns to avoid repetition. Learn personal, possessive, reflexive, interrogative, relative, demonstrative, and distributive pronouns.

content.definition: A pronoun is a word that can replace a noun in a sentence. It helps avoid repetition and makes speech smoother.
key_points: Subject pronouns: I, He, She, We, They, You, It
Object pronouns: Me, Him, Her, Us, Them, You, It
Possessive pronouns: Mine, Yours, His, Hers, Ours, Theirs
Reflexive pronouns: Myself, Himself, Herself, Itself, Themselves
Interrogative pronouns: Who, Whom, Whose, What, Which (5 words only)
Relative pronouns: Who, Which, That, Whose, Whom, Where, When, Why, How
Demonstrative pronouns: This, That, These, Those
Distributive pronouns: Each, Both, Few, Several

patterns.example: He is a teacher.
patterns.explanation: He = subject pronoun replacing a person's name

patterns.example: She helps me.
patterns.explanation: Me = object pronoun receiving the action

patterns.example: The car is mine.
He hurt himself while playing.
patterns.explanation: Himself = subject and object are same

patterns.example: Whose is this bag?
This is the place where I was born.
patterns.explanation: Where = connects to a relative clause

title: Adjectives & Adverbs
summary: Adjectives describe nouns, adverbs modify verbs, adjectives, or other adverbs. Learn their types, order, degrees of comparison, and placement rules.

content.definition: Adjectives modify nouns (answer what kind, which, how many). Adverbs modify verbs, adjectives, or other adverbs (answer how, when, where, how often).
key_points: Attributive adjective comes before noun: 'Few cats like cold water'
Predicative adjective comes after verb: 'The child is asleep'
Adjective order: Opinion → Size → Age → Shape → Colour → Origin → Material → Purpose
Adverb types: manner (quickly), time (yesterday), place (here), frequency (always), degree (very)
Adverb placement: generally close to the word it modifies
Degrees: Positive (tall), Comparative (taller), Superlative (tallest)

patterns.example: She wore a beautiful red dress.
The food tastes delicious.
He speaks fluently.
He never tells a lie.
John is taller than Jim.
He is the tallest in the class.

title: Prepositions & Conjunctions
summary: Prepositions show relationships between words (in, on, at, by). Conjunctions join words, phrases, or clauses (and, but, because, although).

content.definition: Prepositions are words placed before nouns/pronouns to show relationship. Conjunctions are connectors that join words, phrases, or clauses.
key_points: Common prepositions: in, on, at, by, with, for, to, from, about, between
Time prepositions: at (specific time), on (day/date), in (month/year/period)
Place prepositions: at (point), on (surface), in (enclosed space)
Coordinating conjunctions (FANBOYS): For, And, Nor, But, Or, Yet, So
Subordinating conjunctions: because, although, when, while, if, unless, since
Correlative conjunctions: either...or, neither...nor, both...and, not only...but also

patterns.example: He arrives at 5 PM on Monday in January.
The book is on the table in the room.
He is poor but honest.
She stayed because it was raining.
Neither his father nor his mother is happy.
Considering the weather, we stayed home.

title: Tenses — 12 Forms Explained
summary: English has 12 tenses — 4 forms (simple, continuous, perfect, perfect continuous) across 3 times (past, present, future). Master construction and usage.

content.definition: Tenses indicate the time of an action. English has 12 tenses organized in a 3×4 grid: Past/Present/Future × Simple/Continuous/Perfect/Perfect Continuous.
key_points: Simple Present: habitual actions — 'He plays cricket'
Present Continuous: happening now — 'He is playing cricket'
Present Perfect: past action with present relevance — 'He has played cricket'
Present Perfect Continuous: started in past, still continuing — 'He has been playing cricket'
Simple Past: completed action — 'He played cricket'
Past Continuous: was happening — 'He was playing cricket'
Past Perfect: before another past action — 'He had played cricket before lunch'
Simple Future: will happen — 'He will play cricket'
Each tense has 80 sentence variants: Active/Passive × Affirmative/Negative/Interrogative forms

patterns.example: She teaches English every day.
She is teaching English now.
She has taught English for 10 years.
She taught English last year.
She had taught English before she moved.
She will teach English tomorrow.

title: Interrogatives & Imperatives
summary: Questions are critical in spoken English. Learn WH-words, Yes/No questions, alternative questions, question tags, and imperative sentences.

content.definition: Interrogative sentences ask questions. They use WH-words (who, what, where, when, why, which, how) or auxiliary verb inversion. Imperative sentences give commands or requests.
key_points: WH-words: Who (person), What (thing), Where (place), When (time), Why (reason), Which (choice), How (manner)
Yes/No questions start with auxiliary: 'Do you like coffee?'
Question tags confirm: positive statement → negative tag, and vice versa
Alternative questions offer a choice: 'Do you want tea or coffee?'
Imperatives give commands: 'Close the door.' / 'Please sit down.'
Negative imperatives: 'Don't touch that.' / 'Never give up.'

patterns.example: Who is your teacher?
What is your name?
Where do you live?
She is a doctor, isn't she?
They don't like it, do they?
Please close the door.

title: Punctuation Marks
summary: Punctuation gives writing its clarity and rhythm. Learn the full stop, comma, colon, semicolon, apostrophe, quotation marks, and more.

content.definition: Punctuation marks are symbols that organize writing, indicate pauses, show relationships, and clarify meaning. They are essential for written English.
key_points: Full stop (.) — ends a declarative sentence
Comma (,) — separates items in a list, after introductory phrases, in compound sentences
Colon (:) — introduces a list, explanation, or elaboration
Semicolon (;) — connects closely related independent clauses
Apostrophe (') — shows possession (John's) or contraction (don't)
Quotation marks ("") — enclose direct speech or titles
Hyphen (-) — joins compound words: mother-in-law
Dash (—) — adds emphasis or an aside

patterns.example: I bought apples, oranges, and bananas.
patterns.explanation: Separates items in a list (Oxford comma)

patterns.example: He has three hobbies: reading, swimming, and cooking.
She loves reading; he prefers writing.
It's John's car.
English, Monday, January, India
She said, "I will come tomorrow."

title: Active & Passive Voice
summary: In active voice, the subject does the action. In passive voice, the subject receives the action. Learn conversion rules for all tenses.

content.definition: Voice shows whether the subject performs the action (active) or receives it (passive). Passive is formed with be + past participle.
key_points: Active: Subject → Verb → Object: 'She writes a letter'
Passive: Object → be + V3 → by Subject: 'A letter is written by her'
Change object to subject, add 'be' form matching tense, use past participle
Simple Present: writes → is written
Simple Past: wrote → was written
Present Perfect: has written → has been written
Not all sentences can be made passive — intransitive verbs don't have passives

patterns.example: Active: 'He teaches English.' → Passive: 'English is taught by him.'
Active: 'She wrote a letter.' → Passive: 'A letter was written by her.'
Active: 'They are building a house.' → Passive: 'A house is being built.'
Active: 'He has finished the work.' → Passive: 'The work has been finished.'
Active: 'She will sing a song.' → Passive: 'A song will be sung by her.'
Active: 'You can solve this.' → Passive: 'This can be solved by you.'

title: Direct & Indirect Speech
summary: Convert between direct speech (exact words) and indirect/reported speech. Learn the rules for tense shift, pronoun changes, and time/place words.

content.definition: Direct speech quotes the exact words spoken. Indirect (reported) speech conveys the meaning without quoting exact words, requiring changes in tense, pronouns, and time expressions.
key_points: Direct: He said, 'I am happy.' → Indirect: He said that he was happy.
Tense shifts back: am→was, is→was, will→would, can→could
Pronouns change based on speaker: I→he/she, we→they, my→his/her
Time words change: today→that day, tomorrow→the next day, yesterday→the previous day
Questions: 'Where do you live?' → He asked where I lived (no question mark)
Commands: 'Sit down!' → He told me to sit down (use infinitive)

patterns.example: He said, 'I am tired.' → He said that he was tired.
She asked, 'Where do you work?' → She asked where I worked.
He said, 'Close the door.' → He told me to close the door.
She said, 'Please help me.' → She requested me to help her.
'I will come.' → He said he would come.
'I saw him yesterday.' → She said she had seen him the previous day.

title: Conditionals & Wish Clauses
summary: Conditional sentences express if-then relationships. Learn zero, first, second, third conditionals and wish clauses for expressing desires.

content.definition: Conditional sentences have an 'if' clause (condition) and a main clause (result). The type of conditional depends on how real or likely the condition is.
key_points: Zero: If + present, present — general truths: 'If you heat water, it boils'
First: If + present, will + verb — real/likely: 'If it rains, I will stay home'
Second: If + past, would + verb — unreal/unlikely: 'If I were rich, I would travel'
Third: If + past perfect, would have + V3 — impossible (past): 'If I had studied, I would have passed'
Wish + past simple — present wish: 'I wish I were taller'
Wish + past perfect — past regret: 'I wish I had studied harder'

patterns.example: If you heat ice, it melts.
If she studies hard, she will pass.
If I were a bird, I would fly.
If he had come, we would have won.
patterns.explanation: Unreal past — impossible to change now

patterns.example: I wish I knew the answer.
I wish I had listened to her.

title: Degrees of Comparison
summary: Compare things using positive, comparative, and superlative degrees. Learn the rules for -er/-est, more/most, and irregular forms.

content.definition: Degrees of comparison are used to compare adjectives and adverbs. There are three degrees: Positive (no comparison), Comparative (two things), and Superlative (more than two).
key_points: Positive: as + adjective + as — 'John is as strong as Jim'
Comparative: adjective + er/more + than — 'Jim is stronger than John'
Superlative: the + adjective + est/most — 'He is the strongest in the class'
One syllable: tall → taller → tallest
Two+ syllables: beautiful → more beautiful → most beautiful
Irregular: good → better → best; bad → worse → worst

patterns.example: John is as strong as Jim.
She is taller than her sister.
He is the most intelligent student.
No other city is as big as Mumbai.
This is better than that. / This is the best.
She runs faster than him. She runs the fastest.

title: Figures of Speech
summary: Figures of speech add beauty and emphasis to language. Learn simile, metaphor, personification, hyperbole, alliteration, and more.

content.definition: Figures of speech are creative expressions that go beyond literal meaning to add emphasis, beauty, or clarity to language.
key_points: Simile — comparison using 'like' or 'as': 'He is as brave as a lion'
Metaphor — direct comparison without like/as: 'Life is a journey'
Personification — giving human qualities to non-human things: 'The wind whispered'
Hyperbole — exaggeration for effect: 'I've told you a million times'
Alliteration — repetition of initial sounds: 'Peter Piper picked peppers'
Onomatopoeia — words that imitate sounds: 'buzz, hiss, splash'

patterns.example: Her smile was like sunshine.
Time is money.
The flowers danced in the breeze.
I'm so hungry I could eat a horse.
She sells seashells by the seashore.
The bees buzzed around the hive.
